text.title: The 2 Norman principles for per-feature audit
text.summary: Two principles from Don Norman: affordance and feedback. Both are concretely testable per feature; both fix the most common "I clicked it and nothing happened / I did not know I could click it" complaints.

rule.statement: Every clickable / tappable / focusable element MUST visually signal that it is interactive (cursor change, hover state, distinct visual treatment).
rule.rationale: Users do not hunt for hidden affordances. If a tile looks like a card and is also clickable, the click is invisible.

rule.statement: Hover effects MUST be applied ONLY to clickable elements. Decorative tiles MUST NOT hover-respond.
rule.rationale: Hover is an affordance signal — applying it to non-interactive elements lies to the user (memory rule `hover-only-on-clickable`).

rule.statement: Every user action MUST produce visible acknowledgement within 100ms — at minimum a loading indicator, button press state, or focus change.
rule.rationale: Past 100ms the user repeats the click. Repeated clicks cause double-submit bugs and erode trust.

example.note: Submit button: on click, immediately disable + show spinner; on response, swap to success / error state.
Submit button: on click, fire request silently; user clicks three more times waiting for something to happen.
